coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous determination of three herbicide residues in wheat flour based on the hollow fiber supported carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wen-kui
Zhang, Jing
Wang, Song
Ma, Zhi-qing
Feng, Jun-tao
Pei, Hua-wu
Liu, Yu-ming
coredata.dc:description: A solid-phase micro extraction (SPME) coupled high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) strategy was explored for trace concentrations of dicamba, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), and picloram residues simultaneous determination from complex matrix of food samples. The SPME system was established by immobilizing flavonoid moiety incorporated carbon dots (CDs) into the wall-pores of hollow fiber (HF) based on their electrostatic interactions, and as an effective extractant, functional groups incorporated CDs exhibited desirable sensitivity and selectivity to carboxyl contained aromatic herbicides. Associated SPME extraction parameters were investigated, and the proposed SPME-HPLC analytical method was proved to have satisfactory reproducibility with the RSD less than 6.9 %, feasible linearity with the linear coefficients above 0.99, low limits of detection (LOD) that were 0.96, 1.21, and 0.83 ng/g for dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram, desirable accuracy with the recoveries in the range of 76.4–106.9 %. The analytical method has promising potentials for carboxyl contained aromatic herbicides sensitive and selective determination from food samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chromatographic analysis
Pesticide residue
Solid-phase micro extraction
Wheat flour

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SPME comparison between HF and HF-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of CDs (A), the corresponding diameter distribution histograms of CDs (B), FTIR spectrum of CDs (inset showing their fluorescent photo under ultraviolet light at 365 nm) (C), photos of HF and HF-CDs (D), SEM images of HF (E) and HF-CDs (F).
Selectivity evaluation of HF-CDs based on the competitive SPME of seven analytes.
Influences of extraction conditions on SPME of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram, including extraction time (A), solution pH (B), temperature (C), and salt concentration (D).
The typical chromatograms of the extracts from a wheat flour sample and three spiked wheat flour samples with 10, 50 and 100 ng/g of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram.
Determination of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram from spiked wheat flour samples (n = 3).
Comparison of some SPME coupled chromatographic methods for pesticides determination.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous determination of three herbicide residues in wheat flour based on the hollow fiber supported carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Plant Protection, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, 712100, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a College of Plant Protection, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering and Research Center of Biological Pesticides of Shaanxi Province, Yangling, 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering and Research Center of Biological Pesticides of Shaanxi Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b Engineering and Research Center of Biological Pesticides of Shaanxi Province, Yangling 712100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sichuan Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd, CNNC, Guangyuan, 628000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan Environmental Protection Engineering Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: c Sichuan Environmental Protection Engineering co., Ltd, CNNC, Guangyuan, 628000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Plant Protection, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, 712100, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A solid-phase micro extraction (SPME) coupled high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) strategy was explored for trace concentrations of dicamba, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), and picloram residues simultaneous determination from complex matrix of food samples. The SPME system was established by immobilizing flavonoid moiety incorporated carbon dots (CDs) into the wall-pores of hollow fiber (HF) based on their electrostatic interactions, and as an effective extractant, functional groups incorporated CDs exhibited desirable sensitivity and selectivity to carboxyl contained aromatic herbicides. Associated SPME extraction parameters were investigated, and the proposed SPME-HPLC analytical method was proved to have satisfactory reproducibility with the RSD less than 6.9 %, feasible linearity with the linear coefficients above 0.99, low limits of detection (LOD) that were 0.96, 1.21, and 0.83 ng/g for dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram, desirable accuracy with the recoveries in the range of 76.4–106.9 %. The analytical method has promising potentials for carboxyl contained aromatic herbicides sensitive and selective determination from food samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Dicamba, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), and picloram are three hydrophilic herbicides with chlorinated aromatic hydrocarbon structure. They have a range of harmful effects on human health, such as carcinogenicity by inducing of oxidative stress and unfolded protein response (Maciel et al., 2013; Mesnage et al., 2021). The three herbicides are frequently used for weeds control in wheat, and their residues in wheat flour shouldn’t be ignored, which will cause potential damage to human health. Therefore, accurate and sensitive determination of their residues from wheat flour is of great significance, and analytical methods with high sensitivity, low detection limit, superior selectivity, and fast response are desiderated. Chromatographic methods are traditionally used for multi-components pesticide residues determination, however, confronted with complex matrix of food samples, as well as trace or ultra-trace concentration of pesticide residues, the methods encountered increasing technology bottlenecks, and sample preparation methods are therefore urgently needed (Tang et al., 2016).

Solid-phase micro extraction (SPME) is a simple, “green”, efficient, and convenient sample preparation method (Pawliszyn et al., 1997). It employs inert micro extraction support for immobilizing active extractant, the inert support also functions for sample clean-up. Some inert-supports have been employed for SPME, such as stainless steel wire (Chang et al., 2011), microcarrier cytopore (Li et al., 2018), and hollow fiber (HF) (Rahimi et al., 2020). HF can retain the required extractant in its crossed fibroid micropores, and simultaneously prevent the large particles permeating, which has been developed as a promising inert-support for various functional extractants loading, such as metal-organic framework (Liu et al., 2020b), carbon nanomaterials (Darvishnejad et al., 2020; Jagirani and Soylak, 2020; Kazemi et al., 2017), polymers (Barahona et al., 2019; Mirzajani et al., 2020), and others (Bahar et al., 2017; Yazdi et al., 2018).

Carbon dots (CDs) represent a type of emerging multi-functional carbon nanomaterial, which have quasi-spherical shape, small size, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and other unique properties (Song et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2018). Various CDs have been explored on the basis of natural or synthetic carbon precursors (Ding et al., 2014), and some functional types can exhibit desirable separation activity due to their inherited active groups from the precursors or their post-functionalization. Therefore, functional CDs have been increasingly concerned in sample preparation (Chen et al., 2021), for instance, a magnetic CDs/graphene oxide hybrid material was explored for extraction of ibuprofen from human plasma, the introduction of functional material by post-functionalization strategy effectively improved CDs’ extraction ability (Yuvali et al., 2020), a N-doped CDs based SPME platform was established for selective screening and sensitive detection of 2-nitroaniline (Liu et al., 2020a), and a N-doped CDs conjugated with Fe3O4 was explored for magnetic solid phase extraction of lead ions (Mashkani et al., 2018), the introduced nitrogenous groups effectively improved their extraction ability.

Several extraction models have been explored for CDs application in sample preparation, such as Fe3O4 nanoparticles attached magnetic solid phase extraction (Sricharoen et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2018), micro-carrier supported SPME (Li et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2020). Inert-support is indispensable for SPME. Two inert-support, including micro-carrier cytopore and steel wire, have been applied in CDs combination and SPME technology (Li et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2020). As a promising inert-support, HF has not been used in CDs’ micro-extraction, this work therefore focused on HF supported CDs (HF-CDs) construction and SPME application.

Flavonoid moiety incorporated CDs were prepared based on an one-pot hydrothermal strategy by using flavonoid extracts of Ginkgo biloba leaves as the carbon source (Xu et al., 2018). Polypropylene HF was employed as the inert micro-extraction support for immobilization of CDs. HF-CDs was integrated based on the electrostatic interactions, which constituted a novel SPME system. The SPME system was sensitive to carboxyl contained aromatic analytes, and was available for three herbicides including dicamba, 2,4-D and picloram extraction from complex sample matrix. By coupling with high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), SPME-HPLC method was established under the optimized extraction conditions. The analytical method was further employed for the three herbicides simultaneous determination from wheat flour samples.

Dicamba, 2,4-D and picloram were purchased from J&K Chemical Technology (Beijing, China). Benzene, chlorobenzene, 4-aminopyridine, and carboxybenzene were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polypropylene HF membrane (pore size 0.2 μm, internal diameter 600 μm, wall thickness 200 μm) was purchased from Membrana GmbH (Wuppertal, Germany). Fresh Ginkgo biloba leaves were obtained from trees located in Northwest A & F University. All the standard reagents were of chromatographic grade, and general reagents belong to analytical grade. Standard stock solutions of dicamba, 2,4-D and picloram (100.0 μg/mL) were prepared with 50 % methanol-water solution, and required working solutions were freshly prepared by diluting of the stock solutions with 50 % methanol-water solution. All the stock and working solutions were stored at 4 °C.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were observed by a Tecnai-G2-F30 field TEM instrument (FEI, USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were observed on a Nova Nano SEM-450 (FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was measured by an Avatar 330 spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). HPLC system consisted of Waters e2695 system, diode array detector (DAD) and an Agilent C18 column (250 mm × 4.6 mm id, 5 μm). The selected mobile phase was consisted of 60 % methanol and 40 % ultrapure water. The detection wavelengths were 274.6, 229.6 and 222.5 nm for dicamba, 2,4-D and picloram, respectively.

Flavonoid moiety incorporated CDs were prepared according to a reported strategy with some modifications (Xu et al., 2018). Fresh Ginkgo biloba leaves were cleaned, dried and powdered, then 10.0 g of their powder was added into 150 mL of 75 % (V/V) ethanol-water solution. After leaching for 3 h at 65 °C, the mixture was filtered. The filtrate was collected and concentrated with a rotary evaporator, then the flavonoids contained extract of Ginkgo biloba leaves was obtained. The extract was dispersed into 30 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 2 min, then was transferred to a high-temperature reaction kettle for hydrothermal reaction for 6 h at 200 °C. After that, the mixture was centrifuged, and the supernatants were collected and dialyzed for purification with a dialysis membrane (Spectrum, MW cutoff 3000). After freeze-drying, CDs were obtained.

HF was pretreated by cutting into separate segments with the length of each 0.5 cm, immersing into methanol under ultrasonication for 10 min to remove impurities, and drying. CDs suspension was prepared by dispersion of 50.0 mg CDs into 20 mL methanol under ultrasonication. Subsequently, 20 pieces of HFs were simultaneously immersed into the CDs suspension and sonicated for 2 h. After that, CDs loaded HFs were collected, their superficial unstable CDs were removed by repeated washing with methanol and ultrapure water under ultrasonication. Finally, HF-CDs was obtained after drying for 5 h at 60 °C.

A mixed working solution of dicamba, 2,4-D and picloram (each 10.0 μg/mL, pH: 5.2) was prepared for extraction investigation. A piece of HF-CDs was added into 5.0 mL of working solution, then was shaken by a vibrator for 12 min at room temperature. Subsequently, the adsorbed HF-CDs was transferred to an Eppendorf tube, 0.2 mL of acetonitrile was added for desorption, and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min. Desorption operations were repeated for three times, desorption solutions were collected and combined, then 10.0 μL of the desorption solution was employed for HPLC determination.

A mixed solution of benzene, chlorobenzene, 4-aminopyridine, carboxybenzene, dicamba, 2,4-D and picloram was prepared with 50 % methanol-water solution (each 5.0 μg/mL, pH: 5.2). A piece of HF-CDs was transferred to 5.0 mL of the mixed solution to simultaneously extract the above seven analytes. Extraction recoveries of the seven analytes were measured for the extraction selectivity evaluation.

Wheat flour samples were purchased from local markets. One sample (1.0 g) was weighted into 50 mL of 50 % methanol-water solution, then was sonicated for 15 min at 35 °C. Subsequently, the mixture was centrifuged at 1398 xg (Hitachi, CR22 G) for 5 min, the supernatant was collected, and its pH was adjusted to 5.2 for further analysis. The operations were repeated for five batches of wheat flour samples from different manufacturers.

Spiked samples were prepared for method parameters validation, which were prepared by adding certain volumes of standard solutions of three herbicides to the wheat flour samples, followed by repeated above preparation procedures.

The morphology of the prepared CDs was observed by TEM (Fig. 1 A), it can be seen that dispersed CDs presented granular shape with the average diameter of 4.2 nm (Fig. 1B), suggesting CDs were successfully prepared. In addition, considering CDs generally exhibit fluorescence properties, their methanol dispersion solution was observed under ultraviolet light at 365 nm, and bright blue fluorescence can be seen (inset showing in Fig. 1C), further confirming the successful preparation of CDs. The chemical structure of CDs was characterized by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1C, the absorption peak at 3394 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of OH (Ding et al., 2014), and the peak at 2927 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of CH (Wang and Zhou, 2014). Significantly, the peak at 1735 cm−1 was a characteristic absorption peak of CO bond, indicating that flavonoid-associated carbonyl groups were incorporated into the chemical structure of CDs (Xu et al., 2018). In addition, the wavenumber 1546 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic peak of benzene ring, and the peak at 1045 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of COC (Xu et al., 2018), all the information confirmed that flavonoids related moieties had been incorporated into the structure of CDs. The morphology and chemical structure characterizations of CDs were also consistent with the reported work and our previous work (Li et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2018).

The morphology of HF-CDs was characterized. As shown in Fig. 1D, HF-CDs presented darker color as compared to pure white HF, suggesting CDs were incorporated. Besides, the micro-morphologies of HF and HF-CDs were observed by SEM. Fig. 1E and F respectively show the SEM images of the outer walls of HF and HF-CDs, from which it can be observed that the visible pores of HF were reduced after CDs loading, further confirming CDs incorporated HF structure.

The zeta potentials of HF and CDs were measured to be 3.8 mV and -1.6 mV when pH was 5.2 (Fig. S1), suggesting their electrostatic interactions existed. The electrostatic interactions promoted CDs immobilized into the pores of HF.

Competitive SPME was carried out for selectivity evaluation by HF-CDs extraction of a mixed solution consisted of dicamba, 2,4-D, picloram, and a series of characteristic groups contained analytes (benzene, chlorobenzene, 4-aminopyridine, carboxybenzene). Related physical-chemical properties of these analytes were shown in Table S1, and the competitive SPME results were shown in Fig. 2 . It can be seen that all the analytes were more or less extracted, which can be attributed to the aromatic analytes and benzene rings contained CDs induced π-π interactions (Li et al., 2019). Among them, benzene and chlorobenzene were poorly extracted, suggesting that chlorine substituent had negligible contribution to the extraction. Carboxyl and amino contained analytes were better extracted, one carboxyl or amino substituent contained 2,4-D, dicamba, carboxybenzene and 4-aminopyridine were equally extracted, both carboxyl and amino substituents contained picloram was best extracted, suggesting carboxyl and amino substituents were conducive to extraction. These substituents contained analytes had more hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, and larger molecular polar surface area (Table S1), considering flavonoid-related moieties incorporated CDs structure, the possible extraction mechanism can be attributed to the electron-withdrawing carbonyl oxygen from CDs and the electron-donor of carboxyl or amino from analytes resulted hydrogen-bonding interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SPME comparison between HF and HF-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction ability of HF was compared with HF-CDs for SPME of three herbicides. The mixed solution of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram (each 10.0 μg/mL) was employed for the SPME procedures. The results were shown in Fig. S2, it can be seen that HF had negligible extraction abilities for three herbicides as compared to HF-CDs. Therefore, CDs acted as a functional extractant, and HF was proved to be an inert-support. HF-CDs constructed SPME system was proved to be effective for the three herbicides.

HF-CDs constructed SPME system was established under the optimized preparation parameters such as HF length and CDs concentration. Based on the optimized preparation parameters in section 2.3, the obtained HF-CDs exhibited better extraction ability. The critical extraction parameters for the SPME system, including extraction time, solution pH, extraction temperature, salt concentration, and desorption parameters, were further investigated and optimized by extraction of the mixed solution of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram (each 10.0 μg/mL).

SPME investigation have to be carried out with enough time for ensuring extraction equilibriums, therefore, kinetics associated extraction time is critical. Extraction time for dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram was firstly investigated under unadjusted other conditions (pH was 5.2, extraction was at room temperature, and no salt was added) (Fig. 3 A). It can be seen that the extraction equilibriums for three herbicides were attained after 12 min. Therefore, 12 min can be selected as the optimized extraction time for the three herbicides.

Solution pH is a critical parameter influencing the extraction efficiency through changing the chemical forms of both extractant and analytes (Li et al., 2017). Influences of pH on SPME of three herbicides were investigated by controlling pH of the extraction solution from 2 to 9, the optimized extraction time was selected, temperature and salt concentration were unadjusted. It can be seen from Fig. 3B that the extraction recoveries for three herbicides attained the maximums when pH was around 5.2, then the recoveries decreased whatever pH changed. When pH exceeded 5.2, the decreased recoveries can be attributed to the ionization of the carboxyl contained analytes; and when pH below 5.2, the decreased recoveries can be attributed to the protonation of both electron-withdrawing substituents contained herbicides and flavonoids related moieties incorporated CDs. The extra ionization or protonation inhibited the supported hydrogen-bonding interactions, therefore caused the decreased recoveries. Under such circumstances, the optimized extraction pH should be selected around 5.2 for dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram.

Extraction thermodynamics associated temperature was investigated for better SPME of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram. For the investigation, extraction time was 12 min, solution pH was 5.2, and no salt was added, extraction temperature was controlled at 293.15–318.15 K, and the corresponding recoveries were shown in Fig. 3C. It can be seen that the increased temperature slightly promoted the extraction. By comprehensive consideration of the actual operation, room temperature (293.15–298.15 K) was recommended for three herbicides extraction.

Salt concentration influences the extraction by changing the system physical states such as viscosity, ionic strength, and solubility of analytes (Hao et al., 2014). Its influences on extraction of three herbicides were investigated by adding a series of different concentrations of NaCl under the optimized extraction time and solution pH. The result was shown in Fig. 3D, it can be seen that the extraction recoveries were slightly decreased with the increasing salt concentration, which can be attributed to the comprehensive effects of increased ionic strength and viscosity of sample solution, enhanced hydrophobicity of analytes, and decreased solubility of analytes due to salt-out effect (Li et al., 2017). The extra salt addition was unhelpful to three herbicides extraction, which should be avoided.

Desorption parameters were further optimized for dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram. Frequently-used desorption solutions including acetone, methanol and acetonitrile were employed for desorption investigation under ultrasonication, and the corresponding result indicated that acetonitrile brought the maximal desorption recoveries for three herbicides (Fig. S3A). Desorption time was investigated, and 5 min was proved to be the optimized time with the highest recoveries (Fig. S3B). Desorption solution volume was further investigated, and the result indicated that the satisfactory desorption can be attained by eluting the extractant with 0.2 mL acetonitrile thrice (Fig. S3C). Therefore, 0.2 mL acetonitrile was selected for desorption of three herbicides under ultrasonication, desorption time was 5 min, and desorption operations were repeated for three times.

Furthermore, reusability of HF-CDs was investigated. Extracted HF-CDs was desorbed under the optimized conditions, cleaned with ultrapure water and dried, and further reused for its re-extraction ability evaluation. The result indicated that the extraction ability of reused HF-CDs gradually decreased (Fig. S4), which can be attributed to the loss of loaded CDs. Electrostatic interaction supported HF-CDs was still not stable enough, and its extraction recovery reduced by ∼30 % in the fifth extraction. Therefore, reuse of HF-CDs was not recommended more than 5 times. The above optimized SPME conditions were listed in Table S2.

SPME strategy based HPLC method was established by integrating the following optimized conditions. Extraction time was 12 min, solution pH was 5.2, extraction was at room temperature, no salt was added, desorption solution was 0.2 mL acetonitrile, desorption time was 5 min, and desorption operation was repeated for 3 times. Subsequently, the established analytical method was validated by evaluation of analytical parameters.

Firstly, method linearity was evaluated from the calibration curve plotted by SPME of spiked wheat flour samples with six spiked levels of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram in the range of 10.0–1000.0 ng/g, and the linear relationships were attained with the correlation coefficients (r) of 0.992 (dicamba), 0.996 (2,4-D) and 0.990 (picloram), indicating the method linearity was desirable. Secondly, method reproducibility was validated from the analytical precision. Dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram (each 100 ng/g) spiked wheat flour samples were employed for precision evaluation. Intra-day precision was evaluated by six continuous replicate extraction and determination of three herbicides from spiked wheat flour samples on the same day, and inter-day precision was evaluated by six replicate extraction and determination of three herbicides from spiked wheat flour samples on three consecutive days. The obtained relative standard deviation (RSD) values for intra-day and inter-day precision were 1.6–6.9 % and 1.8–6.1 %, indicating the method reproducibility was feasible. Thirdly, the limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of the method were calculated from the corresponding concentrations of analytes at the signal-to-noise ratios of 3 and 10 respectively, the obtained LODs were 0.96, 1.21, and 0.83 ng/g for dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram, and the corresponding LOQs were 3.38, 4.16, and 2.62 ng/g, suggesting the method was sensitive. Fourthly, method accuracy was evaluated by extraction and determination of three herbicides from spiked wheat flour samples with three concentration levels (10, 50 and 100 ng/g). The attained relative recoveries ((found-detected in sample)/spiked×100 %) were in the range of 76.4–106.9 %, and the corresponding RSD was in the range of 1.9–7.6 % (Table 1 ), indicating the method accuracy was satisfactory. The above parameters were summarized in Table S3.

Furthermore, critical parameters of the proposed method were compared with some SPME coupled chromatographic methods for pesticides determination. As seen in Table 2 , the proposed method had efficient extraction time, desirable linear range, less organic solvent, comparable recovery and LODs, further confirming the method was applicable for the three herbicides sensitive and accurate determination.

The analytical method was applied to dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram simultaneous determination from five batches of wheat flour samples. The typical chromatograms of the extracts from a wheat flour sample and three spiked wheat flour samples with 10, 50 and 100 ng/g of dicamba, 2,4-D, and picloram were shown in Fig. 4 . The results indicated that three herbicides were not detected from five batches of wheat flour samples. However, the established analytical method has application potentials for carboxyl contained aromatic herbicides accurate and sensitive determination from complex matrix contained samples.

In the present work, flavonoids moiety incorporated carbon dots (CDs) were prepared as the effective solid-phase micro extraction (SPME) extractant, inert hollow fiber (HF) was employed as the micro-extraction support, and hollow fiber-carbon dots (HF-CDs) micro-extraction system was constructed based on their electrostatic interactions. Functional groups incorporated CDs were proved to be selective to carboxyl contained aromatic analytes based on the possible hydrogen-bonding interactions, HF-CDs based SPME-HPLC method was therefore established for carboxyl contained dicamba, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), and picloram micro-extraction and determination from wheat flour samples. The method had some advantages such as desirable linearity, satisfactory accuracy and sensitivity, low limits of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ), together with feasible selectivity, which has promising application potentials for carboxyl contained aromatic pesticides simultaneous determination from complex matrix contained food samples.

Wen-kui Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jing Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Song Wang: Software, Validation. Zhi-qing Ma: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jun-tao Feng: Resources, Supervision. Hua-wu Pei: Data curation, Investigation. Yu-ming Liu: Investigation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32072447), Shaanxi Provincial Key Research and Development Program of China (2019ZDLNY03-04), and PhD scientific startup foundation of Northwest A&F University (Z1090219020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2022.104426.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Bahar et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An innovative method for analysis of Pb (II) in rice, milk and water samples based on TiO2 reinforced caprylic acid hollow fiber solid/liquid phase microextraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bahar, S., Es'haghi, Z., Nezhadali, A., Banaei, A., Bohlooli, S., 2017. An innovative method for analysis of Pb (II) in rice, milk and water samples based on TiO2 reinforced caprylic acid hollow fiber solid/liquid phase microextraction. Food Chem. 221, 1904-1910.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Barahona et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecularly imprinted polymer-hollow fiber microextraction of hydrophilic fluoroquinolone antibiotics in environmental waters and urine samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Barahona, F., Albero, B., Tadeo, J.L., Martin-Esteban, A., 2019. Molecularly imprinted polymer-hollow fiber microextraction of hydrophilic fluoroquinolone antibiotics in environmental waters and urine samples. J. Chromatogr. A 1587, 42-49.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Cárdenas-Soracá et al., 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of an analytical methodology for the determination of organochlorine pesticides by ethylene-vinyl acetate passive samplers in marine surface waters based on ultrasound-assisted solvent extraction followed with headspace solid-phase microextraction and gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cárdenas-Soracá, D.M., Tucca, F.I., Mardones-Peña, C.A., Barra-Ríos, R.O., 2019. Development of an analytical methodology for the determination of organochlorine pesticides by ethylene-vinyl acetate passive samplers in marine surface waters based on ultrasound-assisted solvent extraction followed with headspace solid-phase microextraction and gas chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry. J. Chromatogr. A 1605, 360341.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chang et al., 2011

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-organic-framework-based tandem molecular sieves as a dual platform for selective microextraction and high-resolution gas chromatographic separation of n-alkanes in complex matrixes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chang, N., Gu, Z.Y., Wang, H.F., Yan, X.P., 2011. Metal-organic-framework-based tandem molecular sieves as a dual platform for selective microextraction and high-resolution gas chromatographic separation of n-alkanes in complex matrixes. Anal. Chem. 83(18), 7094-7101.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots in sample preparation and chromatographic separation: recent advances and future prospects

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, J., Gong, Z., Tang, W., Row, K.H., Qiu, H., 2021. Carbon dots in sample preparation and chromatographic separation: recent advances and future prospects. TrAC Trends Anal. Chem. 134, 116135.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Darvishnejad et al., 2020

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MIL-101 (Cr) @ graphene oxide-reinforced hollow fiber solid-phase microextraction coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography to determine diazinon and chlorpyrifos in tomato, cucumber and agricultural water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Darvishnejad, F., Raoof, J.B., Ghani, M., 2020. MIL-101 (Cr) @ graphene oxide-reinforced hollow fiber solid-phase microextraction coupled with high-performance liquid chromatography to determine diazinon and chlorpyrifos in tomato, cucumber and agricultural water. Anal. Chim. Acta 1140, 99-110.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ding et al., 2014

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ding, H., Wei, J.S., Xiong, H.M., 2014. Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence. Nanoscale 6(22), 13817-13823.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic dispersive micro solid-phase extraction and gas chromatography determination of organophosphorus pesticides in strawberries
